topic: Someone finds their parent's draft letter of resignation from decades ago
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara traced the brittle edges of the parchment with a fingertip, the dust motes dancing in the single shaft of afternoon light cutting across the attic. The letter, yellowed and faintly smelling of aged paper and lavender, was a document from her father. His resignation from the firm, dated thirty years prior. A sudden tightness constricted her chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. She smoothed the paper flat on the trunk, her gaze darting from the elegant, looping script to the stark, formal language. Every word felt like an accusation, a spotlight illuminating a life she never truly understood. She quickly folded it, shoving it deep into the bottom of a box, the movement jerky and hurried. When she finally turned to leave, her shoulders were hunched, a visible attempt to shrink into the shadows of the hallway.

[story 2]

Mark held the envelope as if it were a live wire. The ink was faded, the handwriting familiar yet alien, belonging to his mother. Reading the lines—the polite, almost detached statement of departure—sent a tremor through his hands. He reread the final sentence, his eyes blurring slightly. He kept glancing over his shoulder, half expecting someone to enter the room, ready to snatch the evidence away. A flush crept up his neck, warm and undeniable. He wanted the weight of that past decision to simply vanish, to dissolve into the air. He crumpled the paper once, a small, frustrated sound escaping his throat, before smoothing it out again with meticulous, almost obsessive care, trying to restore some semblance of order to the chaos the discovery had caused inside him.

[story 3]

When Sarah discovered the document tucked inside a forgotten book, a strange paralysis seized her. The letter detailed her uncle’s departure, a neat, professional severance from a life she only knew through fragmented, idealized family stories. She sat back on her heels, gripping her knees so tightly her knuckles turned white. Her breathing became shallow, quick little catches in her throat. She couldn't look at the paper for long; the words seemed to vibrate with unspoken history. She started pacing the small room, her movements becoming restless and jerky, unable to settle her gaze on the evidence of a life she was suddenly measuring against her own, finding the gap between them painfully wide.
